# Exodus Chapter 30 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~51r–51v (PDF pp. 101–102); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 73–74 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_030_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_030_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_030_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Altar of Incense (vv. 1–10)

**1** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] an altar [**mizbeyach**] for the burning of [**miqtar**] incense [**qetoret**]; of acacia [**atsay shittim**] wood [**ta'aseh**] you shall make [**oto**] it.

**2** A cubit [**ammah**] its length [**orko**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] its width [**rochbo**] — square [**ravua'**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] — and two [**ve'ammatayim**] cubits [**qomato**] its height; from it [**mimmennu**] shall be its horns [**qarnotav**].

**3** And you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] it [**oto**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] — its top [**et-gaggo**] and its walls [**ve'et-qirotav**] all around [**saviv**] and its horns [**ve'et-qarnotav**] — and you shall make [**ve'asita**] for it [**lo**] a molding [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**4** And two [**ushtey**] rings [**tab'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] for it [**lo**] beneath [**mittachat**] its molding [**lezero**]; on [**al**] its two [**shtey**] sides [**tsela'otav**] you shall make them [**ta'aseh**] on its two [**al-shney**] flanks [**tsiddav**]; and they shall be [**vehayah**] holders [**lebattim**] for poles [**levaddim**] to carry [**lase'et**] it [**oto**] by them [**bahem**].

**5** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] of acacia [**atsay shittim**] wood, and you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**].

**6** And you shall place [**venatattha**] it [**oto**] before [**lifney**] the veil [**happarokhet**] that [**asher**] is over [**al**] the Ark of [**aron**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**], before [**lifney**] the mercy seat [**hakkaporet**] that [**asher**] is over [**al**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**], where [**asher**] I will meet [**ivva'ed**] with you [**lekha**] there [**shammah**].

**7** And Aaron [**vehiqtir**] shall burn [**alav**] on it [**qetoret**] fragrant incense [**sammim**]; morning by [**babboqer**] morning [**babboqer**], when he tends [**beheytivo**] the lamps [**et-hannerot**], he shall burn it [**yaqtirenah**].

**8** And when Aaron [**uveha'alot**] lights [**Aharon**] the lamps [**et-hannerot**] between [**beyn**] the two evenings [**ha'arbayim**], he shall burn it [**yaqtirenah**] — a perpetual [**tamid**] incense [**qetoret**] before [**lifney**] YHWH throughout your generations [**ledorotheyekhem**].

**9** You shall not [**lo**] offer [**ta'alu**] upon it [**alav**] unauthorized [**zarah**] incense [**qetoret**], nor burnt offering [**ve'olah**], nor grain offering [**uminchah**]; and a drink offering [**venesekh**] you shall not [**lo**] pour [**tisskekhu**] upon it [**alav**].

**10** And Aaron [**vekhipper Aharon**] shall make atonement [**al**] upon [**qarnotav**] its horns [**achat**] once [**bashanah**] in the year; with the blood of [**middam**] the sin offering of [**chattat**] atonement [**hakkippurim**], once [**achat**] in the year [**bashanah**] he shall make atonement [**yekhapper**] upon it [**alav**] throughout your generations [**ledorotheyekhem**]; it is most holy [**qodesh qodashim**] it [**hu**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**]."

---

## The Census Tax — The Half Shekel (vv. 11–16)

**11** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**12** "When [**ki**] you take [**tissa**] the sum [**et-rosh**] of the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] according to those who are numbered [**lifqudeyhem**] of them, then each man shall give [**venat'nu**] a ransom for [**kofer**] his soul [**nafsho**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**] when you number [**bifqod**] them [**otam**], that there be no [**velo-yihyeh**] plague [**negef**] among them [**bahem**] when you number [**bifqod**] them [**otam**].

**13** This [**zeh**] they shall give [**yitt'nu**] — every one [**kol**] who passes over [**ha'over**] to [**al**] those who are numbered [**happequdim**] — a half [**machatsit**] shekel [**hasheqel**] according to the shekel of [**besheqel**] the sanctuary [**haqqodesh**] (twenty [**esrim**] gerahs [**gerah**] the shekel [**hasheqel**]); a half [**machatsit**] shekel [**hasheqel**] as a contribution [**terumah**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**14** Every one [**kol**] who passes over [**ha'over**] to [**al**] those who are numbered [**happequdim**], from a son of [**mibben**] twenty [**esrim**] years [**shanah**] and upward [**vama'lah**], shall give [**yitten**] the contribution [**terumat**] of YHWH [**YHWH**].

**15** The rich [**he'ashir**] shall not [**lo**] give more [**yarbeh**] and the poor [**vehadal**] shall not [**lo**] give less [**yam'it**] than the half [**mimachatsit**] shekel [**hasheqel**], when giving [**latet**] the contribution [**et-terumat**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] to make atonement [**lekhapper**] for [**al**] your souls [**nafshotheyekhem**].

**16** And you shall take [**velaqachta**] the silver of [**et-kesef**] the atonements [**hakkippurim**] from [**me'et**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] and you shall assign [**venatattha**] it [**oto**] for [**al**] the service of [**avodat**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**]; and it shall be [**vehayah**] for the Sons of Israel [**livney Yisra'el**] as a memorial [**lezikkaron**] before [**lifney**] YHWH, to make atonement [**lekhapper**] for [**al**] your souls [**nafshotheyekhem**]."

---

## The Bronze Basin (vv. 17–21)

**17** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**18** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a basin [**kiyyor**] of bronze [**nechoshet**] and its stand [**vekanno**] of bronze [**nechoshet**], for washing [**lerachtsah**]; and you shall place [**venatattha**] it [**oto**] between [**beyn**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] and the altar [**uveyn hammizbeyach**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] water [**shammah mayim**] there in it.

**19** And Aaron [**verachatsu**] and his sons [**Aharon uvanav**] shall wash [**mimmennu**] from it [**et-yedeyhem**] their hands [**ve'et-ragleyhem**] and their feet.

**20** When they go [**bevo'am**] into [**el**] the Tent of [**ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] they shall wash [**yirchatsu**] with water [**mayim**] and they shall not [**velo**] die [**yamutu**], or [**o**] when they draw near [**begishtam**] to [**el**] the altar [**hammizbeyach**] to minister [**leshareth**], to burn [**lehaqtir**] an offering by fire [**ishsheh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**21** And they shall wash [**verachatsu**] their hands [**yedeyhem**] and their feet [**veragleyhem**] and they shall not [**velo**] die [**yamutu**]; and it shall be [**vehay'tah**] for them [**lahem**] a perpetual [**olam**] statute [**choq**], for him [**lo**] and for his seed [**ulzar'o**] throughout their generations [**ledorotam**]."

---

## The Holy Anointing Oil (vv. 22–33)

**22** And YHWH spoke [**vayedabber YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**], saying [**le'mor**]:

**23** "And you [**ve'attah**], take [**qach**] for yourself [**lekha**] spices [**besamim**] of the finest [**rosh**]: flowing myrrh [**mor-deror**] five hundred [**chamesh me'ot**] shekels, and fragrant cinnamon [**veqinnemon-besem**] half [**machatsito**] of that — two hundred and fifty [**chamishim umata'yim**] — and fragrant cane [**uqneh-vosem**] two hundred and fifty [**chamishim umata'yim**],

**24** and cassia [**veqiddah**] five hundred [**chamesh me'ot**] according to the shekel of [**besheqel**] the sanctuary [**haqqodesh**], and olive [**zayit**] oil [**veshemen**] a hin [**hin**].

**25** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] it [**oto**] an oil of [**shemen**] holy [**mishchat-qodesh**] anointing [**roqach**], a perfume mixture [**mirqachat**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a perfumer [**roqeach**]; an oil of [**shemen**] holy [**mishchat-qodesh**] anointing [**yihyeh**] it shall be.

**26** And you shall anoint [**umashachta**] with it [**vo**] the Tent of [**et-ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] and the Ark of [**ve'et aron**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**],

**27** and the table [**ve'et-hashulchan**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its utensils [**kelav**], and the lampstand [**ve'et-hammenorah**] and its utensils [**ve'et-keleyha**], and the altar of [**ve'et mizbeyach**] incense [**haqqetoret**],

**28** and the altar of [**ve'et mizbeyach**] burnt offering [**ha'olah**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its utensils [**kelav**], and the basin [**ve'et-hakkiyyor**] and its stand [**ve'et-kanno**].

**29** And you shall consecrate [**veqiddashta**] them [**otam**] and they shall be [**vehayu**] most holy [**qodesh qodashim**]; everything [**kol**] that touches [**hannogea**] them [**bahem**] shall be holy [**yiqdash**].

**30** And Aaron [**ve'et-Aharon**] and his sons [**ve'et-banav**] you shall anoint [**timshach**], and you shall consecrate [**veqiddashta**] them [**otam**] to minister as priests [**lekhaheno**] to Me [**li**].

**31** And to [**ve'el**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] you shall speak [**tedabber**], saying [**le'mor**]: 'An oil of [**shemen**] holy [**mishchat-qodesh**] anointing [**yihyeh**] this shall be [**zeh**] to Me [**li**] throughout your generations [**ledorotheyekhem**].

**32** Upon [**al**] the flesh [**besar**] of a man [**adam**] it shall not [**lo**] be poured [**yissakh**], and according to its proportion [**uvematkuneto**] you shall not [**lo**] make [**ta'asu**] any like it [**kamohu**]; it [**hu**] is holy [**qodesh**], holy [**qodesh**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] to you [**lakhem**].

**33** A man [**ish**] who [**asher**] mixes [**yirqach**] like it [**kamohu**] or who [**va'asher**] puts [**yitten**] of it [**mimmennu**] upon [**al**] a stranger [**zar**] shall be cut off [**venikhrat**] from his people [**me'ammav**].' "

---

## The Holy Incense (vv. 34–38)

**34** And YHWH said [**vayyomer YHWH**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: "Take [**qach**] for yourself [**lekha**] spices [**sammim**] — stacte [**nataf**] and onycha [**ushchelet**] and galbanum [**vechelbenah**] — spices [**sammim**] and pure [**zakh**] frankincense [**ulevonah**]; part [**bad**] for part [**bevad**] it shall be [**yihyeh**].

**35** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] it [**otah**] an incense [**qetoret**], a perfume mixture [**roqach**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a perfumer [**roqeach**], salted [**memullach**], pure [**tahor**], holy [**qodesh**].

**36** And you shall grind [**veshachaqta**] some of it [**mimmennah**] fine [**hadeq**], and you shall put [**venatattha**] some of it [**mimmennah**] before [**lifney**] the Testimony [**ha'edut**] in the Tent of [**be'ohel**] Meeting [**mo'ed**] where [**asher**] I will meet [**ivva'ed**] with you [**lekha**] there [**shammah**]; most holy [**qodesh qodashim**] it shall be [**tihyeh**] to you [**lakhem**].

**37** And the incense [**vehaqqetoret**] which [**asher**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**], according to its proportion [**bematkunetah**] you shall not [**lo**] make [**ta'asu**] for yourselves [**lakhem**]; holy [**qodesh**] it shall be [**tihyeh**] to you [**lekha**] for YHWH [**laYHWH**].

**38** A man [**ish**] who [**asher**] makes [**ya'aseh**] like it [**kamohah**] to smell [**leharich**] it shall be cut off [**venikhrat**] from his people [**me'ammav**]."

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**mizbeyach miqtar qetoret** (v. 1) -- "altar for the burning of incense." The incense altar is distinct from the great bronze altar of burnt offering (mizbeyach ha'olah, 27:1-8) in material (acacia wood overlaid with gold rather than bronze), size (one cubit square, two cubits high -- far smaller than the five-cubit-square burnt offering altar), location (inside the Tabernacle proper, before the veil, rather than in the outer court), and function (burning fragrant incense rather than animal sacrifices). The term miqtar is a noun from q-t-r ("to burn, cause to rise in smoke"), specifically designating the burning of aromatic substances as distinct from the general burning of sacrifices. The incense altar occupies the most sacred position of any furnishing outside the Holy of Holies, standing directly before the veil that screens the Ark of the Testimony. Its placement between the lampstand and the table of the Presence, facing the mercy seat, positions it as the liturgical focus of priestly ministry within the Holy Place. The LXX renders this as thusiastērion thumiatou thumiamatos ("altar of incense-burning of incense"), using the technical term thusiastērion that the Septuagint consistently reserves for YHWH's altars (as distinct from bōmos, used for pagan altars). The author of Hebrews refers to the incense altar with the term thusiastērion tou thumiamatos (Heb 9:4 variant reading) or thumiaterion ("censer" or "incense altar"), and its relationship to the Holy of Holies remains one of the great interpretive questions of that epistle.

**qetoret** (vv. 1, 7, 8, 9, 27, 34, 35, 36, 37) -- "incense." The noun qetoret (from q-t-r, "to rise in smoke") designates the aromatic compound burned on the golden altar. The incense is described as qetoret sammim (v. 7, "incense of spices" or "fragrant incense"), indicating that it is not simply any smoke but a carefully compounded aromatic preparation. The four ingredients specified in v. 34 -- nataf (stacte, a resinous gum), shchelet (onycha, from a mollusk), chelbenah (galbanum, a pungent resin), and levonah zakh (pure frankincense) -- are to be mixed in equal parts and prepared by a perfumer (roqeach). The burning of incense twice daily, morning and evening (vv. 7-8), synchronized with the tending and lighting of the lamps, creates a liturgical pairing: light and fragrance together fill the Holy Place, representing the dual sensory dimension of divine worship. The prohibition against "unauthorized incense" (qetoret zarah, v. 9) will find its tragic narrative fulfillment in Leviticus 10:1, where Nadab and Abihu offer "unauthorized fire" (esh zarah) and are consumed. In the prophetic tradition, incense becomes a metaphor for prayer ascending to God (Ps 141:2, "Let my prayer be set before You as incense"), a connection made explicit in Revelation 5:8 and 8:3-4, where the golden bowls of incense are identified as "the prayers of the saints." The LXX renders qetoret as thumiama ("incense, aromatic offering").

**kofer** (v. 12) -- "ransom." The noun kofer (from k-p-r, "to cover, atone, ransom") designates a payment made to redeem a life that would otherwise be forfeit. The census tax of the half shekel is explicitly called a kofer nafsho ("ransom for his soul/life"), indicating that the act of numbering the people carries an inherent danger -- a negef ("plague, striking") may result from the census if the ransom is not paid (v. 12). The theological logic is that each Israelite life belongs to YHWH, and the act of counting the people constitutes a kind of mustering or claiming that encroaches upon divine prerogative. The kofer acknowledges YHWH's ultimate ownership of each life. David's census in 2 Samuel 24, conducted without the kofer provision, resulted in precisely the plague that Exodus 30:12 warns against. The half-shekel amount -- identical for rich and poor (v. 15) -- declares the equal worth of every Israelite life before YHWH: no soul costs more or less to ransom than another. The LXX renders kofer as lutra ("ransom price"), cognate with the verb lutroō ("to ransom, redeem") that becomes central to New Testament soteriology (Mark 10:45; 1 Tim 2:6; Titus 2:14; 1 Pet 1:18).

**kiyyor** (v. 18) -- "basin" or "laver." The noun kiyyor designates the bronze washing vessel placed between the Tent of Meeting and the altar of burnt offering. Unlike the other Tabernacle furnishings, the kiyyor receives remarkably sparse description -- no dimensions are given, and no decorative details are specified. Only its material (bronze), its stand (kanno, also bronze), and its function (washing the hands and feet of the priests) are stated. This minimalism contrasts sharply with the elaborate descriptions of the ark, table, lampstand, and altars. The kiyyor's function is purely preparatory and purificatory: the priests must wash before entering the Tent or approaching the altar, and the consequence of failure is death (vv. 20-21). The washing is not hygienic but ritual -- it marks the transition from the profane to the sacred realm, the crossing of a boundary that requires purification. In Exodus 38:8, the kiyyor is said to have been made from the mirrors of the serving women, a detail that adds a dimension of self-reflection to the purification symbolism. The LXX renders kiyyor as loutēr ("washing basin, laver"), and the ritual washing it prescribes stands behind the Christian practice of baptism as priestly preparation for worship and service.

**shemen mishchat-qodesh** (vv. 25, 31) -- "oil of holy anointing" or "holy anointing oil." The compound phrase designates the sacred oil compounded from four spices in an olive oil base. The spices are mor-deror ("flowing myrrh," the finest grade that drips naturally from the tree), qinnemon-besem ("fragrant cinnamon"), qneh-vosem ("fragrant cane" or "aromatic calamus"), and qiddah ("cassia"). The quantities are substantial -- 500 shekels of myrrh, 250 of cinnamon, 250 of aromatic cane, 500 of cassia, in a hin (approximately six liters) of olive oil -- indicating a large preparation intended for the entire consecration of the Tabernacle and its furnishings as well as the priestly succession. The term mashach ("to anoint") is the verbal root from which mashiach ("anointed one, messiah") derives. The anointing oil consecrates everything it touches: the Tabernacle, the Ark, the table, the lampstand, both altars, the basin, and finally Aaron and his sons (vv. 26-30). The strict prohibition against reproducing the formula for common use (vv. 32-33) and the penalty of karet ("cutting off") for violation underscore its sacred character. The LXX renders this as elaion chrisma hagiasmon ("oil of anointing of sanctification"), and the concept of sacred anointing becomes the defining metaphor of messianic kingship and priesthood throughout the biblical tradition (1 Sam 16:13; Ps 2:2; Isa 61:1; Luke 4:18; Acts 10:38).

### Grammatical Notes

**ki tissa et-rosh beney Yisra'el** (v. 12) -- "When you take the head/sum of the Sons of Israel." The idiom nasa' et-rosh ("to lift the head") is a distinctive census formula meaning "to take a count" or "to take a census" (cf. Num 1:2, 49; 4:2, 22; 26:2). Literally "to lift the head," the expression may derive from the practice of having people raise their heads to be counted, or from the metaphorical sense of "elevating" or "singling out" each individual for enumeration. The same idiom carries a second, contrasting meaning in Genesis 40:13, 19-20, where "lifting the head" of the chief butler signifies restoration, while "lifting the head from upon" the chief baker signifies execution -- both involving a kind of individual reckoning. The census idiom thus carries overtones of divine reckoning: to be counted is to be individually distinguished before YHWH, which is both a privilege and a peril. The LXX renders this as ean labēs ton sunlogismon tōn huiōn Israēl ("when you take the reckoning of the sons of Israel"), using sunlogismos ("reckoning, calculation").

**bad bevad yihyeh** (v. 34) -- "part for part it shall be," meaning in equal proportions. The noun bad (from b-d-d, "to be separate, alone") here means "a part, a portion." The expression bad bevad specifies that each of the four incense ingredients is to be measured in equal quantity. This equalizing formula is unique in the Torah and applies only to the sacred incense -- no such requirement of equal proportions appears for the anointing oil. The equal-parts formulation may carry symbolic weight: no single ingredient dominates the sacred fragrance, just as no single aspect of worship or prayer overshadows the others. The LXX renders this as ison isō estai ("equal to equal it shall be"), making the proportional equality explicit.

**memullach tahor qodesh** (v. 35) -- "salted, pure, holy." This triad of adjectives describes the completed incense compound. The participle memullach (from m-l-ch, "to salt") is the Hophal participle ("salted, treated with salt"), indicating that salt is added to the incense mixture. Salt in the ancient Near Eastern cultic context is a preservative and a covenant symbol (cf. Lev 2:13, "the salt of the covenant of your God"; Num 18:19, "a covenant of salt"). The salting of the incense connects it to the broader covenantal theology of the Tabernacle: the fragrance that rises before YHWH is a covenant offering, preserved and sealed by salt. The three terms form an ascending sequence -- from physical preparation (salted) to ritual status (pure) to sacred designation (holy) -- mirroring the progressive consecration of the Tabernacle's furnishings and personnel. The LXX renders memullach as memimenon or summigeon ("mixed"), apparently interpreting the salting as a general mixing or compounding process rather than a specific addition of salt.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 30 is often regarded as an appendix or supplement to the main Tabernacle instructions of chapters 25-29, and its placement has generated considerable scholarly discussion. The incense altar, the bronze basin, the anointing oil, and the sacred incense are all essential components of the Tabernacle worship system, yet they appear after the ordination instructions of chapter 29 rather than with the other furnishings in chapters 25-27. Several explanations have been proposed: (1) these items are placed here because they presuppose the priestly ordination just described -- the incense altar requires a consecrated priest to serve it, the basin requires priests who must wash, and the anointing oil requires a priesthood to anoint; (2) the chapter moves from the most sacred item (the incense altar, nearest to the Holy of Holies) to the most peripheral (the bronze basin in the outer court), then to the consecrating substances (oil and incense), creating a thematic rather than spatial sequence; (3) the LXX of Exodus preserves a significantly different arrangement of material in chapters 35-40, suggesting that the ordering of Tabernacle instructions was fluid in the textual tradition.

The five sections of the chapter (incense altar, census tax, bronze basin, anointing oil, sacred incense) are linked by the theme of atonement and consecration. The incense altar is the site of annual atonement (v. 10); the census tax is a kofer ("ransom") that effects kippurim ("atonements," v. 16); the basin provides the ritual purification without which the priests die (vv. 20-21); the anointing oil consecrates every sacred object and person (vv. 26-30); and the sacred incense fills the Holy Place with the fragrance that represents Israel's prayers ascending to God. Together, these five elements establish the conditions under which the priestly worship described in Leviticus can proceed: the sanctuary must be fragrant, the people ransomed, the priests purified, and every surface consecrated.

The census tax section (vv. 11-16) introduces an important socio-theological principle: the equality of all Israelites before YHWH. The half-shekel ransom is identical for rich and poor (v. 15), establishing that the value of a human life in the divine economy cannot be measured by wealth. This egalitarian principle stands in tension with the graduated scale of Leviticus 27:1-8, where the valuation of persons dedicated to YHWH varies by age and sex. The distinction is that the Leviticus 27 valuations concern voluntary dedications (the economic productivity that the person's absence would cost the sanctuary), whereas the Exodus 30 half-shekel concerns the inherent worth of the life itself -- and in YHWH's eyes, every life is equally costly to ransom.

### Theological Notes

The incense altar (vv. 1-10) occupies a theologically liminal position. It stands before the veil, in the Holy Place, yet it is oriented toward the Holy of Holies and the mercy seat beyond. On the Day of Atonement, its horns receive the blood of the sin offering (v. 10; cf. Lev 16:18-19), connecting it directly to the most sacred rite of the liturgical year. The twice-daily burning of incense creates a continuous aromatic presence before YHWH -- morning and evening, the Holy Place is filled with fragrant smoke. This synchronization with the lamp-tending (vv. 7-8) means that the priest's daily ministry in the Holy Place involves two primary actions: maintaining light and generating fragrance. These are the two sensory expressions of worship that can penetrate the veil -- light that illuminates toward the Presence and fragrance that ascends toward the Presence. The prohibition against offering any other substance on the incense altar (v. 9) -- no burnt offering, no grain offering, no drink offering -- preserves its singular function: it exists only for the fragrant communion between the priest and YHWH across the veil.

The theological significance of the bronze basin (vv. 17-21) lies in its death sanction. The instruction "they shall wash... and they shall not die" (vv. 20-21) implies its converse: failure to wash results in death. This is one of only a few Tabernacle instructions that carry an explicit death penalty for procedural violation (cf. 28:35, 43). The basin thus marks the boundary between the profane and the sacred as a lethal boundary -- a threshold that cannot be crossed casually. The priest must be ritually clean to enter the sacred space or to approach the altar, and the cleansing agent is water from the bronze basin. This ritual washing stands at the head of a biblical trajectory that runs through Elisha's command to Naaman to wash in the Jordan (2 Kgs 5:10), through Ezekiel's vision of water flowing from the temple (Ezek 47:1-12), through John's baptism in the Jordan as priestly preparation for the Messiah, to Jesus's washing of the disciples' feet (John 13:1-17), where the priestly washing is inverted: the High Priest washes His servants rather than being washed.

The anointing oil (vv. 22-33) and the sacred incense (vv. 34-38) share a common structure: each is a carefully compounded mixture, prepared by a professional perfumer (roqeach), restricted to sacred use, and protected by the penalty of karet ("cutting off from the people") for unauthorized reproduction. This parallel structure emphasizes that both substances are set apart (qodesh) in the most absolute sense -- they belong to YHWH alone and may not be appropriated for human pleasure or purposes. The anointing oil consecrates by contact: everything it touches becomes qodesh qodashim ("most holy"), and everything that subsequently touches those consecrated objects also becomes holy (v. 29). This contagious holiness radiates outward from the oil through the furnishings to the priests to the worship itself, creating concentric circles of consecration centered on the divine Presence. The sacred incense similarly fills the Holy Place with a fragrance that belongs to YHWH alone -- a scent that no Israelite may reproduce for personal enjoyment. The exclusivity of these substances declares that the experience of YHWH's presence -- the anointing that consecrates and the fragrance that ascends -- cannot be manufactured or simulated by human initiative. They are given by divine prescription and reserved for divine encounter.

The annual atonement on the incense altar's horns (v. 10) connects this chapter directly to the Day of Atonement ritual of Leviticus 16. The phrase qodesh qodashim hu laYHWH ("it is most holy to YHWH") -- applied to the incense altar -- is the same designation given to the most sacred offerings and spaces, placing this small golden altar in the highest category of holiness despite its location outside the Holy of Holies. The LXX renders v. 10 with characteristic precision: exilasetai Aarōn epi tōn keratōn autou hapax tou eniautou ("Aaron shall make propitiation upon its horns once in the year"), using the verb exilaskomai ("to propitiate, make atonement") that becomes the basis for the New Testament concept of hilasmos and hilastērion (1 John 2:2; Rom 3:25).

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 30 (pages 73-74) begins in the middle of page 73, marked with the section number KA (= 21) in the left margin of the central column, indicating the LXX's chapter division. The text corresponds closely to the MT but with several notable features. At v. 1, the LXX renders mizbeyach miqtar qetoret as thusiastērion thumiamatos ("altar of incense"), using the standard Septuagintal term for YHWH's altar. At v. 6, the incense altar's placement is described as plēsion tou katapetasmatos ("near the veil"), corresponding to the MT's lifney happarokhet. At v. 10, the annual atonement is rendered exilasetai Aarōn epi tōn keratōn autou hapax tou eniautou apo tou haimatos tou katharismou ("Aaron shall make propitiation upon its horns once in the year from the blood of purification"), where katharismos ("purification") interprets the MT's chattat hakkippurim. At v. 12, the census formula ean labēs ton sunlogismon tōn huiōn Israēl ("when you take the reckoning of the sons of Israel") renders the MT's ki tissa et-rosh beney Yisra'el, replacing the literal "lifting of the head" with the more interpretive "reckoning." At v. 13, the half shekel is rendered to hēmisu tou didrachmou ("half of the didrachma"), converting the Hebrew weight measure into the Greek monetary equivalent -- a conversion that stands behind the "temple tax" (didrachma) of Matthew 17:24-27. At v. 15, the rich/poor equality provision is rendered ho ploutōn ou prosthēsei kai ho penomenos ouk elattonēsei ("the rich shall not add and the poor shall not diminish"). At v. 18, the basin is rendered loutēra chalkoun ("bronze laver"). At v. 23, the anointing oil ingredients are rendered with Greek spice terminology: smurna eklektē ("choice myrrh"), kinamōmon euōdes ("fragrant cinnamon"), kalamos euōdes ("fragrant cane"), and iris/kasia for cassia. At v. 34, the incense ingredients are staktēn ("stacte/drops"), onucha ("onycha"), chalbanēn ("galbanum"), and libanon diaphanē ("transparent frankincense"), with the equal-proportions formula rendered ison isō estai ("equal to equal it shall be"). The Vaticanus manuscript continues the chapter onto page 74, where the anointing oil and incense instructions conclude. The transition to chapter 31 (marked KE = chapter 25 in the LXX section numbering of Vaticanus) appears in the right column of page 74.

--- LOGOS
